stimulus:
The following text is from a 1906 short story by Aurelia Whitmore, “The House at the Crossing,” set in a New England river town referred to as the town of M.

If visitors to the small river town of M. observed aloud that its winters were severe and its diversions few, the townspeople would reply that, on the contrary, M. was a place of much excellent society, that it had a lending library and a singing school, that lectures were given at the meeting-house through January and February, and finally, that quite a number of agreeable households lived there with whom an acquaintance might be cultivated. And they always named the Crowders as the most cultivated and most public-spirited household of them all.
stem: What does the text suggest about the Crowders?

answerOptions.content: They are amused by the manners of other households in the town of M.
They have recently moved into the town of M.
They occupy a distinctive position among the households of the town of M.
They have long disliked living in the town of M.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The text says that several agreeable households live in M. and that townspeople always single out the Crowders as the “most cultivated and most public-spirited” of them all, which suggests that the Crowders hold a distinctive position among the town’s households.

Choice A is incorrect because the text describes how the Crowders are viewed by others, not how they view other residents. Choice B is incorrect because the text gives no information about when the Crowders arrived in M. Choice D is incorrect because the text reveals nothing about the Crowders’ feelings toward life in M.
